    23→// Plan is the top-level type representing a planned set of changes.
    24→//
    25→// A plan is a summary of the set of changes required to move from a current
    26→// state to a goal state derived from configuration. The described changes
    27→// are not applied directly, but contain an approximation of the final
    28→// result that will be completed during apply by resolving any values that
    29→// cannot be predicted.
    30→//
    31→// A plan must always be accompanied by the configuration it was built from,
    32→// since the plan does not itself include all of the information required to
    33→// make the changes indicated.
    34→type Plan struct {
    35→	// Mode is the mode under which this plan was created.
    36→	//
    37→	// This is only recorded to allow for UI differences when presenting plans
    38→	// to the end-user, and so it must not be used to influence apply-time
    39→	// behavior. The actions during apply must be described entirely by
    40→	// the Changes field, regardless of how the plan was created.
    41→	//
    42→	// FIXME: destroy operations still rely on DestroyMode being set, because
    43→	// there is no other source of this information in the plan. New behavior
    44→	// should not be added based on this flag, and changing the flag should be
    45→	// checked carefully against existing destroy behaviors.
    46→	UIMode Mode
    47→
    48→	// VariableValues, VariableMarks, and ApplyTimeVariables together describe
    49→	// how Terraform should decide the input variable values for the apply
    50→	// phase if this plan is to be applied.
    51→	//
    52→	// VariableValues and VariableMarks describe persisted (non-ephemeral)
    53→	// values that were set as part of the planning options and are to be
    54→	// re-used during the apply phase. VariableValues can potentially contain
    55→	// unknown values for a speculative plan, but the variable values must
    56→	// all be known for a plan that will subsequently be applied.
    57→	//
    58→	// ApplyTimeVariables retains the names of any ephemeral variables that were
    59→	// set (non-null) during the planning phase and must therefore be
    60→	// re-supplied by the caller (potentially with different values) during
    61→	// the apply phase. Ephemeral input variables are intended for populating
    62→	// arguments for other ephemeral objects in the configuration, such as
    63→	// provider configurations. Although the values for these variables can
    64→	// change between plan and apply, their "nullness" may not.
    65→	VariableValues     map[string]DynamicValue
    66→	VariableMarks      map[string][]cty.PathValueMarks
    67→	ApplyTimeVariables collections.Set[string]
    68→
    69→	Changes                   *ChangesSrc
    70→	DriftedResources          []*ResourceInstanceChangeSrc
    71→	DeferredResources         []*DeferredResourceInstanceChangeSrc
    72→	DeferredActionInvocations []*DeferredActionInvocationSrc
    73→	TargetAddrs               []addrs.Targetable
    74→	ActionTargetAddrs         []addrs.Targetable
    75→	ForceReplaceAddrs         []addrs.AbsResourceInstance
    76→
    77→	Backend    *Backend
    78→	StateStore *StateStore
    79→
    80→	// Complete is true if Terraform considers this to be a "complete" plan,
    81→	// which is to say that it includes a planned action (even if no-op)
    82→	// for every resource instance object that was mentioned across both
    83→	// the desired state and prior state.
    84→	//
    85→	// If Complete is false then the plan might still be applyable (check
    86→	// [Plan.Applyable]) but after applying it the operator should be reminded
    87→	// to plan and apply again to hopefully make more progress towards
    88→	// convergence.
    89→	//
    90→	// For an incomplete plan, other fields of this type may give more context
    91→	// about why the plan is incomplete, which a UI layer could present to
    92→	// the user as part of a warning that the plan is incomplete.
    93→	Complete bool
    94→
    95→	// Applyable is true if both Terraform was able to create a plan
    96→	// successfully and if the plan calls for making some sort of meaningful
    97→	// change.
    98→	//
    99→	// If [Plan.Errored] is also set then that means the plan is non-applyable
   100→	// due to an error. If not then the plan was created successfully but found
   101→	// no material differences between desired and prior state, and so
   102→	// applying this plan would achieve nothing.
   103→	Applyable bool
   104→
   105→	// Errored is true if the Changes information is incomplete because
   106→	// the planning operation failed. An errored plan cannot be applied,
   107→	// but can be cautiously inspected for debugging purposes.
   108→	Errored bool
   109→
   110→	// Checks captures a snapshot of the (probably-incomplete) check results
   111→	// at the end of the planning process.
   112→	//
   113→	// If this plan is applyable (that is, if the planning process completed
   114→	// without errors) then the set of checks here should be complete even
   115→	// though some of them will likely have StatusUnknown where the check
   116→	// condition depends on values we won't know until the apply step.
   117→	Checks *states.CheckResults
   118→
   119→	// RelevantAttributes is a set of resource instance addresses and
   120→	// attributes that are either directly affected by proposed changes or may
   121→	// have indirectly contributed to them via references in expressions.
   122→	//
   123→	// This is the result of a heuristic and is intended only as a hint to
   124→	// the UI layer in case it wants to emphasize or de-emphasize certain
   125→	// resources. Don't use this to drive any non-cosmetic behavior, especially
   126→	// including anything that would be subject to compatibility constraints.
   127→	RelevantAttributes []globalref.ResourceAttr
   128→
   129→	// PrevRunState and PriorState both describe the situation that the plan
   130→	// was derived from:
   131→	//
   132→	// PrevRunState is a representation of the outcome of the previous
   133→	// Terraform operation, without any updates from the remote system but
   134→	// potentially including some changes that resulted from state upgrade
   135→	// actions.
   136→	//
   137→	// PriorState is a representation of the current state of remote objects,
   138→	// which will differ from PrevRunState if the "refresh" step returned
   139→	// different data, which might reflect drift.
   140→	//
   141→	// PriorState is the main snapshot we use for actions during apply.
   142→	// PrevRunState is only here so that we can diff PriorState against it in
   143→	// order to report to the user any out-of-band changes we've detected.
   144→	PrevRunState *states.State
   145→	PriorState   *states.State
   146→
   147→	// ExternalReferences are references that are being made to resources within
   148→	// the plan from external sources.
   149→	//
   150→	// This is never recorded outside of Terraform. It is not written into the
   151→	// binary plan file, and it is not written into the JSON structured outputs.
   152→	// The testing framework never writes the plans out but holds everything in
   153→	// memory as it executes, so there is no need to add any kind of
   154→	// serialization for this field. This does mean that you shouldn't rely on
   155→	// this field existing unless you have just generated the plan.
   156→	ExternalReferences []*addrs.Reference
   157→
   158→	// Overrides contains the set of overrides that were applied while making
   159→	// this plan. We need to provide the same set of overrides when applying
   160→	// the plan so we preserve them here. As with  ExternalReferences, this is
   161→	// only used by the testing framework and so isn't written into any external
   162→	// representation of the plan.
   163→	Overrides *mocking.Overrides
   164→
   165→	// Timestamp is the record of truth for when the plan happened.
   166→	Timestamp time.Time
   167→
   168→	// FunctionResults stores hashed results from all providers function calls
   169→	// and builtin calls which may access external state so that calls during
   170→	// apply can be checked for consistency.
   171→	FunctionResults []lang.FunctionResultHash
   172→}
   173→
   174→// ProviderAddrs returns a list of all of the provider configuration addresses
   175→// referenced throughout the receiving plan.
   176→//
   177→// The result is de-duplicated so that each distinct address appears only once.
   178→func (p *Plan) ProviderAddrs() []addrs.AbsProviderConfig {
   179→	if p == nil || p.Changes == nil {
   180→		return nil
   181→	}
   182→
   183→	m := map[string]addrs.AbsProviderConfig{}
   184→
   185→	// Get all provider requirements from resources.
   186→	for _, rc := range p.Changes.Resources {
   187→		m[rc.ProviderAddr.String()] = rc.ProviderAddr
   188→	}
   189→
   190→	// Get the provider required for pluggable state storage, if that's in use.
   191→	//
   192→	// This check should be redundant as:
   193→	// 1) Any provider used for state storage would be in required_providers, which is checked separately elsewhere.
   194→	// 2) An apply operation that uses the planfile only checks the providers needed for the plan _after_ the operations backend
   195→	//    for the operation is set up, and that process will detect if the provider needed for state storage is missing.
   196→	//
   197→	// However, for completeness when describing the providers needed by a plan, it is included here.
   198→	if p.StateStore != nil {
   199→		address := addrs.AbsProviderConfig{
   200→			Module:   addrs.RootModule, // A state_store block is only ever in the root module
   201→			Provider: *p.StateStore.Provider.Source,
   202→			// Alias: aliases are not permitted when using a provider for PSS.
   203→		}
   204→
   205→		m[p.StateStore.Provider.Source.String()] = address
   206→	}
   207→
   208→	if len(m) == 0 {
   209→		return nil
   210→	}
   211→
   212→	// This is mainly just so we'll get stable results for testing purposes.
   213→	keys := make([]string, 0, len(m))
   214→	for k := range m {
   215→		keys = append(keys, k)
   216→	}
   217→	sort.Strings(keys)
   218→
   219→	ret := make([]addrs.AbsProviderConfig, len(keys))
   220→	for i, key := range keys {
   221→		ret[i] = m[key]
   222→	}
   223→
   224→	return ret
   225→}

   227→// Backend represents the backend-related configuration and other data as it
   228→// existed when a plan was created.
   229→type Backend struct {
   230→	// Type is the type of backend that the plan will apply against.
   231→	Type string
   232→
   233→	// Config is the configuration of the backend, whose schema is decided by
   234→	// the backend Type.
   235→	Config DynamicValue
   236→
   237→	// Workspace is the name of the workspace that was active when the plan
   238→	// was created. It is illegal to apply a plan created for one workspace
   239→	// to the state of another workspace.
   240→	// (This constraint is already enforced by the statefile lineage mechanism,
   241→	// but storing this explicitly allows us to return a better error message
   242→	// in the situation where the user has the wrong workspace selected.)
   243→	Workspace string
   244→}
   245→
   246→func NewBackend(typeName string, config cty.Value, configSchema *configschema.Block, workspaceName string) (*Backend, error) {
   247→	dv, err := NewDynamicValue(config, configSchema.ImpliedType())
   248→	if err != nil {
   249→		return nil, err
   250→	}
   251→
   252→	return &Backend{
   253→		Type:      typeName,
   254→		Config:    dv,
   255→		Workspace: workspaceName,
   256→	}, nil
   257→}
   258→
   259→// StateStore represents the state store-related configuration and other data as it
   260→// existed when a plan was created.
   261→type StateStore struct {
   262→	// Type is the type of state store that the plan will apply against.
   263→	Type string
   264→
   265→	Provider *Provider
   266→
   267→	// Config is the configuration of the state store, whose schema is obtained
   268→	// from the host provider's GetProviderSchema response.
   269→	Config DynamicValue
   270→
   271→	// Workspace is the name of the workspace that was active when the plan
   272→	// was created. It is illegal to apply a plan created for one workspace
   273→	// to the state of another workspace.
   274→	// (This constraint is already enforced by the statefile lineage mechanism,
   275→	// but storing this explicitly allows us to return a better error message
   276→	// in the situation where the user has the wrong workspace selected.)
   277→	Workspace string
   278→}
   279→
   280→type Provider struct {
   281→	Version *version.Version // The specific provider version used for the state store. Should be set using a getproviders.Version, etc.
   282→	Source  *tfaddr.Provider // The FQN/fully-qualified name of the provider.
   283→
   284→	// Config is the configuration of the state store, whose schema is obtained
   285→	// from the host provider's GetProviderSchema response.
   286→	Config DynamicValue
   287→}
   288→
   289→func NewStateStore(typeName string, ver *version.Version, source *tfaddr.Provider, storeConfig cty.Value, storeSchema *configschema.Block, providerConfig cty.Value, providerSchema *configschema.Block, workspaceName string) (*StateStore, error) {
   290→	sdv, err := NewDynamicValue(storeConfig, storeSchema.ImpliedType())
   291→	if err != nil {
   292→		return nil, err
   293→	}
   294→	pdv, err := NewDynamicValue(providerConfig, providerSchema.ImpliedType())
   295→	if err != nil {
   296→		return nil, err
   297→	}
   298→
   299→	provider := &Provider{
   300→		Version: ver,
   301→		Source:  source,
   302→		Config:  pdv,
   303→	}
   304→
   305→	return &StateStore{
   306→		Type:      typeName,
   307→		Provider:  provider,
   308→		Config:    sdv,
   309→		Workspace: workspaceName,
   310→	}, nil
   311→}

   313→// SetVersion includes logic for parsing a string representation of a version,
   314→// for example data read from a plan file.
   315→// If an error occurs it is returned and the receiver's Version field is unchanged.
   316→// If there are no errors then the receiver's Version field is updated.
   317→func (p *Provider) SetVersion(input string) error {
   318→	ver, err := version.NewVersion(input)
   319→	if err != nil {
   320→		return err
   321→	}
   322→
   323→	p.Version = ver
   324→	return nil
   325→}

   327→// SetSource includes logic for parsing a string representation of a provider source,
   328→// for example data read from a plan file.
   329→// If an error occurs it is returned and the receiver's Source field is unchanged.
   330→// If there are no errors then the receiver's Source field is updated.

   334→		return diags.ErrWithWarnings()
   335→	}
   336→
   337→	p.Source = &source

    20→// Changes describes various actions that Terraform will attempt to take if
    21→// the corresponding plan is applied.
    22→type Changes struct {
    23→	// Resources tracks planned changes to resource instance objects.
    24→	Resources []*ResourceInstanceChange
    25→
    26→	Queries []*QueryInstance
    27→
    28→	// ActionInvocations tracks planned action invocations, which may have
    29→	// embedded resource instance changes.
    30→	ActionInvocations ActionInvocationInstances
    31→
    32→	// Outputs tracks planned changes output values.
    33→	//
    34→	// Note that although an in-memory plan contains planned changes for
    35→	// outputs throughout the configuration, a plan serialized
    36→	// to disk retains only the root outputs because they are
    37→	// externally-visible, while other outputs are implementation details and
    38→	// can be easily re-calculated during the apply phase. Therefore only root
    39→	// module outputs will survive a round-trip through a plan file.
    40→	Outputs []*OutputChange
    41→}
    42→
    43→// NewChanges returns a valid Changes object that describes no changes.
    44→func NewChanges() *Changes {
    45→	return &Changes{}
    46→}
    47→
    48→// Encode encodes all the stored resource and output changes into a new *ChangeSrc value
    49→func (c *Changes) Encode(schemas *schemarepo.Schemas) (*ChangesSrc, error) {
    50→	// a plan is always built even when there are errors, so make sure to return
    51→	// a valid changesSrc.
    52→	changesSrc := NewChangesSrc()
    53→
    54→	for _, rc := range c.Resources {
    55→		p, ok := schemas.Providers[rc.ProviderAddr.Provider]
    56→		if !ok {
    57→			return changesSrc, fmt.Errorf("Changes.Encode: missing provider %s for %s", rc.ProviderAddr, rc.Addr)
    58→		}
    59→
    60→		var schema providers.Schema
    61→		switch rc.Addr.Resource.Resource.Mode {
    62→		case addrs.ManagedResourceMode:
    63→			schema = p.ResourceTypes[rc.Addr.Resource.Resource.Type]
    64→		case addrs.DataResourceMode:
    65→			schema = p.DataSources[rc.Addr.Resource.Resource.Type]
    66→		case addrs.ListResourceMode:
    67→			schema = p.ListResourceTypes[rc.Addr.Resource.Resource.Type]
    68→		default:
    69→			panic(fmt.Sprintf("unexpected resource mode %s", rc.Addr.Resource.Resource.Mode))
    70→		}
    71→
    72→		if schema.Body == nil {
    73→			return changesSrc, fmt.Errorf("Changes.Encode: missing schema for %s", rc.Addr)
    74→		}
    75→		rcs, err := rc.Encode(schema)
    76→		if err != nil {
    77→			return changesSrc, fmt.Errorf("Changes.Encode: %w", err)
    78→		}
    79→
    80→		changesSrc.Resources = append(changesSrc.Resources, rcs)
    81→	}
    82→
    83→	for _, qi := range c.Queries {
    84→		p, ok := schemas.Providers[qi.ProviderAddr.Provider]
    85→		if !ok {
    86→			return changesSrc, fmt.Errorf("Changes.Encode: missing provider %s for %s", qi.ProviderAddr, qi.Addr)
    87→		}
    88→
    89→		schema := p.ListResourceTypes[qi.Addr.Resource.Resource.Type]
    90→		if schema.Body == nil {
    91→			return changesSrc, fmt.Errorf("Changes.Encode: missing schema for %s", qi.Addr)
    92→		}
    93→		rcs, err := qi.Encode(schema)
    94→		if err != nil {
    95→			return changesSrc, fmt.Errorf("Changes.Encode: %w", err)
    96→		}
    97→
    98→		changesSrc.Queries = append(changesSrc.Queries, rcs)
    99→	}
   100→
   101→	for _, ai := range c.ActionInvocations {
   102→		p, ok := schemas.Providers[ai.ProviderAddr.Provider]
   103→		if !ok {
   104→			return changesSrc, fmt.Errorf("Changes.Encode: missing provider %s for %s", ai.ProviderAddr, ai.Addr)
   105→		}
   106→		schema, ok := p.Actions[ai.Addr.Action.Action.Type]
   107→		if !ok {
   108→			return changesSrc, fmt.Errorf("Changes.Encode: missing schema for %s", ai.Addr.Action.Action.Type)
   109→		}
   110→		a, err := ai.Encode(&schema)
   111→		if err != nil {
   112→			return changesSrc, fmt.Errorf("Changes.Encode: %w", err)
   113→		}
   114→		changesSrc.ActionInvocations = append(changesSrc.ActionInvocations, a)
   115→	}
   116→
   117→	for _, ocs := range c.Outputs {
   118→		oc, err := ocs.Encode()
   119→		if err != nil {
   120→			return changesSrc, err
   121→		}
   122→		changesSrc.Outputs = append(changesSrc.Outputs, oc)
   123→	}
   124→
   125→	return changesSrc, nil
   126→}

   210→// RootOutputValues returns planned changes for all outputs of the root module.
   211→func (c *Changes) RootOutputValues() []*OutputChange {
   212→	var res []*OutputChange
   213→
   214→	for _, oc := range c.Outputs {
   215→		// we can't evaluate root module outputs
   216→		if !oc.Addr.Module.Equal(addrs.RootModuleInstance) {
   217→			continue
   218→		}
   219→
   220→		res = append(res, oc)
   221→
   222→	}
   223→
   224→	return res
   225→}

   227→// OutputValues returns planned changes for all outputs for all module
   228→// instances that reside in the parent path.  Returns nil if no changes are
   229→// planned.
   230→func (c *Changes) OutputValues(parent addrs.ModuleInstance, module addrs.ModuleCall) []*OutputChange {
   231→	var res []*OutputChange
   232→
   233→	for _, oc := range c.Outputs {
   234→		// we can't evaluate root module outputs
   235→		if oc.Addr.Module.Equal(addrs.RootModuleInstance) {
   236→			continue
   237→		}
   238→
   239→		changeMod, changeCall := oc.Addr.Module.Call()
   240→		// this does not reside on our parent instance path
   241→		if !changeMod.Equal(parent) {
   242→			continue
   243→		}
   244→
   245→		// this is not the module you're looking for
   246→		if changeCall.Name != module.Name {
   247→			continue
   248→		}
   249→
   250→		res = append(res, oc)
   251→
   252→	}
   253→
   254→	return res
   255→}

   325→// ResourceInstanceChange describes a change to a particular resource instance
   326→// object.
   327→type ResourceInstanceChange struct {
   328→	// Addr is the absolute address of the resource instance that the change
   329→	// will apply to.
   330→	Addr addrs.AbsResourceInstance
   331→
   332→	// PrevRunAddr is the absolute address that this resource instance had at
   333→	// the conclusion of a previous run.
   334→	//
   335→	// This will typically be the same as Addr, but can be different if the
   336→	// previous resource instance was subject to a "moved" block that we
   337→	// handled in the process of creating this plan.
   338→	//
   339→	// For the initial creation of a resource instance there isn't really any
   340→	// meaningful "previous run address", but PrevRunAddr will still be set
   341→	// equal to Addr in that case in order to simplify logic elsewhere which
   342→	// aims to detect and react to the movement of instances between addresses.
   343→	PrevRunAddr addrs.AbsResourceInstance
   344→
   345→	// DeposedKey is the identifier for a deposed object associated with the
   346→	// given instance, or states.NotDeposed if this change applies to the
   347→	// current object.
   348→	//
   349→	// A Replace change for a resource with create_before_destroy set will
   350→	// create a new DeposedKey temporarily during replacement. In that case,
   351→	// DeposedKey in the plan is always states.NotDeposed, representing that
   352→	// the current object is being replaced with the deposed.
   353→	DeposedKey states.DeposedKey
   354→
   355→	// Provider is the address of the provider configuration that was used
   356→	// to plan this change, and thus the configuration that must also be
   357→	// used to apply it.
   358→	ProviderAddr addrs.AbsProviderConfig
   359→
   360→	// Change is an embedded description of the change.
   361→	Change
   362→
   363→	// ActionReason is an optional extra indication of why we chose the
   364→	// action recorded in Change.Action for this particular resource instance.
   365→	//
   366→	// This is an approximate mechanism only for the purpose of explaining the
   367→	// plan to end-users in the UI and is not to be used for any
   368→	// decision-making during the apply step; if apply behavior needs to vary
   369→	// depending on the "action reason" then the information for that decision
   370→	// must be recorded more precisely elsewhere for that purpose.
   371→	//
   372→	// Sometimes there might be more than one reason for choosing a particular
   373→	// action. In that case, it's up to the codepath making that decision to
   374→	// decide which value would provide the most relevant explanation to the
   375→	// end-user and return that. It's not a goal of this field to represent
   376→	// fine details about the planning process.
   377→	ActionReason ResourceInstanceChangeActionReason
   378→
   379→	// RequiredReplace is a set of paths that caused the change action to be
   380→	// Replace rather than Update. Always nil if the change action is not
   381→	// Replace.
   382→	//
   383→	// This is retained only for UI-plan-rendering purposes and so it does not
   384→	// currently survive a round-trip through a saved plan file.
   385→	RequiredReplace cty.PathSet
   386→
   387→	// Private allows a provider to stash any extra data that is opaque to
   388→	// Terraform that relates to this change. Terraform will save this
   389→	// byte-for-byte and return it to the provider in the apply call.
   390→	Private []byte
   391→}

   393→// Encode produces a variant of the receiver that has its change values
   394→// serialized so it can be written to a plan file. Pass the implied type of the
   395→// corresponding resource type schema for correct operation.
   396→func (rc *ResourceInstanceChange) Encode(schema providers.Schema) (*ResourceInstanceChangeSrc, error) {
   397→	cs, err := rc.Change.Encode(&schema)
   398→	if err != nil {
   399→		return nil, err
   400→	}
   401→	prevRunAddr := rc.PrevRunAddr
   402→	if prevRunAddr.Resource.Resource.Type == "" {
   403→		// Suggests an old caller that hasn't been properly updated to
   404→		// populate this yet.
   405→		prevRunAddr = rc.Addr
   406→	}
   407→	return &ResourceInstanceChangeSrc{
   408→		Addr:            rc.Addr,
   409→		PrevRunAddr:     prevRunAddr,
   410→		DeposedKey:      rc.DeposedKey,
   411→		ProviderAddr:    rc.ProviderAddr,
   412→		ChangeSrc:       *cs,
   413→		ActionReason:    rc.ActionReason,
   414→		RequiredReplace: rc.RequiredReplace,
   415→		Private:         rc.Private,
   416→	}, err
   417→}

   419→func (rc *ResourceInstanceChange) DeepCopy() *ResourceInstanceChange {
   420→	if rc == nil {
   421→		return rc
   422→	}
   423→
   424→	ret := *rc
   425→	return &ret
   426→}

   428→func (rc *ResourceInstanceChange) Moved() bool {
   429→	return !rc.Addr.Equal(rc.PrevRunAddr)
   430→}

   432→// Simplify will, where possible, produce a change with a simpler action than
   433→// the receiver given a flag indicating whether the caller is dealing with
   434→// a normal apply or a destroy. This flag deals with the fact that Terraform
   435→// Core uses a specialized graph node type for destroying; only that
   436→// specialized node should set "destroying" to true.
   437→//
   438→// The following table shows the simplification behavior:
   439→//
   440→//	Action    Destroying?   New Action
   441→//	--------+-------------+-----------
   442→//	Create    true          NoOp
   443→//	Delete    false         NoOp
   444→//	Replace   true          Delete
   445→//	Replace   false         Create
   446→//
   447→// For any combination not in the above table, the Simplify just returns the
   448→// receiver as-is.
   449→func (rc *ResourceInstanceChange) Simplify(destroying bool) *ResourceInstanceChange {
   450→	if destroying {
   451→		switch rc.Action {
   452→		case Delete:
   453→			// We'll fall out and just return rc verbatim, then.
   454→		case CreateThenDelete, DeleteThenCreate:
   455→			return &ResourceInstanceChange{
   456→				Addr:         rc.Addr,
   457→				DeposedKey:   rc.DeposedKey,
   458→				Private:      rc.Private,
   459→				ProviderAddr: rc.ProviderAddr,
   460→				Change: Change{
   461→					Action:          Delete,
   462→					Before:          rc.Before,
   463→					BeforeIdentity:  rc.BeforeIdentity,
   464→					After:           cty.NullVal(rc.Before.Type()),
   465→					AfterIdentity:   cty.NullVal(rc.BeforeIdentity.Type()),
   466→					Importing:       rc.Importing,
   467→					GeneratedConfig: rc.GeneratedConfig,
   468→				},
   469→			}
   470→		default:
   471→			return &ResourceInstanceChange{
   472→				Addr:         rc.Addr,
   473→				DeposedKey:   rc.DeposedKey,
   474→				Private:      rc.Private,
   475→				ProviderAddr: rc.ProviderAddr,
   476→				Change: Change{
   477→					Action:          NoOp,
   478→					Before:          rc.Before,
   479→					BeforeIdentity:  rc.BeforeIdentity,
   480→					After:           rc.Before,
   481→					AfterIdentity:   rc.BeforeIdentity,
   482→					Importing:       rc.Importing,
   483→					GeneratedConfig: rc.GeneratedConfig,
   484→				},
   485→			}
   486→		}
   487→	} else {
   488→		switch rc.Action {
   489→		case Delete:
   490→			return &ResourceInstanceChange{
   491→				Addr:         rc.Addr,
   492→				DeposedKey:   rc.DeposedKey,
   493→				Private:      rc.Private,
   494→				ProviderAddr: rc.ProviderAddr,
   495→				Change: Change{
   496→					Action:          NoOp,
   497→					Before:          rc.Before,
   498→					BeforeIdentity:  rc.BeforeIdentity,
   499→					After:           rc.Before,
   500→					AfterIdentity:   rc.BeforeIdentity,
   501→					Importing:       rc.Importing,
   502→					GeneratedConfig: rc.GeneratedConfig,
   503→				},
   504→			}
   505→		case CreateThenDelete, DeleteThenCreate:
   506→			return &ResourceInstanceChange{
   507→				Addr:         rc.Addr,
   508→				DeposedKey:   rc.DeposedKey,
   509→				Private:      rc.Private,
   510→				ProviderAddr: rc.ProviderAddr,
   511→				Change: Change{
   512→					Action:          Create,
   513→					Before:          cty.NullVal(rc.After.Type()),
   514→					BeforeIdentity:  cty.NullVal(rc.AfterIdentity.Type()),
   515→					After:           rc.After,
   516→					AfterIdentity:   rc.AfterIdentity,
   517→					Importing:       rc.Importing,
   518→					GeneratedConfig: rc.GeneratedConfig,
   519→				},
   520→			}
   521→		}
   522→	}
   523→
   524→	// If we fall out here then our change is already simple enough.
   525→	return rc
   526→}

   528→// ResourceInstanceChangeActionReason allows for some extra user-facing
   529→// reasoning for why a particular change action was chosen for a particular
   530→// resource instance.
   531→//
   532→// This only represents sufficient detail to give a suitable explanation to
   533→// an end-user, and mustn't be used for any real decision-making during the
   534→// apply step.
   535→type ResourceInstanceChangeActionReason rune
   536→
   537→//go:generate go tool golang.org/x/tools/cmd/stringer -type=ResourceInstanceChangeActionReason changes.go
   538→
   539→const (
   540→	// In most cases there's no special reason for choosing a particular
   541→	// action, which is represented by ResourceInstanceChangeNoReason.
   542→	ResourceInstanceChangeNoReason ResourceInstanceChangeActionReason = 0
   543→
   544→	// ResourceInstanceReplaceBecauseTainted indicates that the resource
   545→	// instance must be replaced because its existing current object is
   546→	// marked as "tainted".
   547→	ResourceInstanceReplaceBecauseTainted ResourceInstanceChangeActionReason = 'T'
   548→
   549→	// ResourceInstanceReplaceByRequest indicates that the resource instance
   550→	// is planned to be replaced because a caller specifically asked for it
   551→	// to be using ReplaceAddrs. (On the command line, the -replace=...
   552→	// planning option.)
   553→	ResourceInstanceReplaceByRequest ResourceInstanceChangeActionReason = 'R'
   554→
   555→	// ResourceInstanceReplaceByTriggers indicates that the resource instance
   556→	// is planned to be replaced because of a corresponding change in a
   557→	// replace_triggered_by reference.
   558→	ResourceInstanceReplaceByTriggers ResourceInstanceChangeActionReason = 'D'
   559→
   560→	// ResourceInstanceReplaceBecauseCannotUpdate indicates that the resource
   561→	// instance is planned to be replaced because the provider has indicated
   562→	// that a requested change cannot be applied as an update.
   563→	//
   564→	// In this case, the RequiredReplace field will typically be populated on
   565→	// the ResourceInstanceChange object to give information about specifically
   566→	// which arguments changed in a non-updatable way.
   567→	ResourceInstanceReplaceBecauseCannotUpdate ResourceInstanceChangeActionReason = 'F'
   568→
   569→	// ResourceInstanceDeleteBecauseNoResourceConfig indicates that the
   570→	// resource instance is planned to be deleted because there's no
   571→	// corresponding resource configuration block in the configuration.
   572→	ResourceInstanceDeleteBecauseNoResourceConfig ResourceInstanceChangeActionReason = 'N'
   573→
   574→	// ResourceInstanceDeleteBecauseWrongRepetition indicates that the
   575→	// resource instance is planned to be deleted because the instance key

   497→func (c *Context) refreshOnlyPlan(config *configs.Config, prevRunState *states.State, opts *PlanOpts) (*plans.Plan, *lang.Scope, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
   498→	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
   499→
   500→	if opts.Mode != plans.RefreshOnlyMode {
   501→		panic(fmt.Sprintf("called Context.refreshOnlyPlan with %s", opts.Mode))
   502→	}
   503→
   504→	plan, evalScope, walkDiags := c.planWalk(config, prevRunState, opts)
   505→	diags = diags.Append(walkDiags)
   506→	if diags.HasErrors() {
   507→		// Non-nil plan along with errors indicates a non-applyable partial
   508→		// plan that's only suitable to be shown to the user as extra context
   509→		// to help understand the errors.
   510→		return plan, evalScope, diags
   511→	}
   512→
   513→	// If the graph builder and graph nodes correctly obeyed our directive
   514→	// to refresh only, the set of resource changes should always be empty.
   515→	// We'll safety-check that here so we can return a clear message about it,
   516→	// rather than probably just generating confusing output at the UI layer.
   517→	if len(plan.Changes.Resources) != 0 {
   518→		// Some extra context in the logs in case the user reports this message
   519→		// as a bug, as a starting point for debugging.
   520→		for _, rc := range plan.Changes.Resources {
   521→			if depKey := rc.DeposedKey; depKey == states.NotDeposed {
   522→				log.Printf("[DEBUG] Refresh-only plan includes %s change for %s", rc.Action, rc.Addr)
   523→			} else {
   524→				log.Printf("[DEBUG] Refresh-only plan includes %s change for %s deposed object %s", rc.Action, rc.Addr, depKey)
   525→			}
   526→		}
   527→		diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
   528→			tfdiags.Error,
   529→			"Invalid refresh-only plan",
   530→			"Terraform generated planned resource changes in a refresh-only plan. This is a bug in Terraform.",
   531→		))
   532→	}
   533→
   534→	// We don't populate RelevantResources for a refresh-only plan, because
   535→	// they never have any planned actions and so no resource can ever be
   536→	// "relevant" per the intended meaning of that field.
   537→
   538→	return plan, evalScope, diags
   539→}

   541→func (c *Context) destroyPlan(config *configs.Config, prevRunState *states.State, opts *PlanOpts) (*plans.Plan, *lang.Scope, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
   542→	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
   543→
   544→	if opts.Mode != plans.DestroyMode {
   545→		panic(fmt.Sprintf("called Context.destroyPlan with %s", opts.Mode))
   546→	}
   547→
   548→	priorState := prevRunState
   549→
   550→	// A destroy plan starts by running Refresh to read any pending data
   551→	// sources, and remove missing managed resources. This is required because
   552→	// a "destroy plan" is only creating delete changes, and is essentially a
   553→	// local operation.
   554→	//
   555→	// NOTE: if skipRefresh _is_ set then we'll rely on the destroy-plan walk
   556→	// below to upgrade the prevRunState and priorState both to the latest
   557→	// resource type schemas, so NodePlanDestroyableResourceInstance.Execute
   558→	// must coordinate with this by taking that action only when c.skipRefresh
   559→	// _is_ set. This coupling between the two is unfortunate but necessary
   560→	// to work within our current structure.
   561→	if !opts.SkipRefresh && !prevRunState.Empty() {
   562→		log.Printf("[TRACE] Context.destroyPlan: calling Context.plan to get the effect of refreshing the prior state")
   563→		refreshOpts := *opts
   564→		refreshOpts.Mode = plans.NormalMode
   565→		refreshOpts.PreDestroyRefresh = true
   566→		refreshOpts.OverridePreventDestroy = false
   567→
   568→		// FIXME: A normal plan is required here to refresh the state, because
   569→		// the state and configuration may not match during a destroy, and a
   570→		// normal refresh plan can fail with evaluation errors. In the future
   571→		// the destroy plan should take care of refreshing instances itself,
   572→		// where the special cases of evaluation and skipping condition checks
   573→		// can be done.
   574→		refreshPlan, _, refreshDiags := c.plan(config, prevRunState, &refreshOpts)
   575→		if refreshDiags.HasErrors() {
   576→			// NOTE: Normally we'd append diagnostics regardless of whether
   577→			// there are errors, just in case there are warnings we'd want to
   578→			// preserve, but we're intentionally _not_ doing that here because
   579→			// if the first plan succeeded then we'll be running another plan
   580→			// in DestroyMode below, and we don't want to double-up any
   581→			// warnings that both plan walks would generate.
   582→			// (This does mean we won't show any warnings that would've been
   583→			// unique to only this walk, but we're assuming here that if the
   584→			// warnings aren't also applicable to a destroy plan then we'd
   585→			// rather not show them here, because this non-destroy plan for
   586→			// refreshing is largely an implementation detail.)
   587→			diags = diags.Append(refreshDiags)
   588→			return nil, nil, diags
   589→		}
   590→
   591→		// We'll use the refreshed state -- which is the  "prior state" from
   592→		// the perspective of this "destroy plan" -- as the starting state
   593→		// for our destroy-plan walk, so it can take into account if we
   594→		// detected during refreshing that anything was already deleted outside
   595→		// of Terraform.
   596→		priorState = refreshPlan.PriorState.DeepCopy()
   597→
   598→		// The refresh plan may have upgraded state for some resources, make
   599→		// sure we store the new version.
   600→		prevRunState = refreshPlan.PrevRunState.DeepCopy()
   601→		log.Printf("[TRACE] Context.destroyPlan: now _really_ creating a destroy plan")
   602→	}
   603→
   604→	destroyPlan, evalScope, walkDiags := c.planWalk(config, priorState, opts)
   605→	diags = diags.Append(walkDiags)
   606→	if walkDiags.HasErrors() {
   607→		// Non-nil plan along with errors indicates a non-applyable partial
   608→		// plan that's only suitable to be shown to the user as extra context
   609→		// to help understand the errors.
   610→		return destroyPlan, evalScope, diags
   611→	}
   612→
   613→	if !opts.SkipRefresh {
   614→		// If we didn't skip refreshing then we want the previous run state to
   615→		// be the one we originally fed into the c.refreshOnlyPlan call above,
   616→		// not the refreshed version we used for the destroy planWalk.
   617→		destroyPlan.PrevRunState = prevRunState
   618→	}
   619→
   620→	relevantAttrs, rDiags := c.relevantResourceAttrsForPlan(config, destroyPlan)
   621→	diags = diags.Append(rDiags)
   622→
   623→	destroyPlan.RelevantAttributes = relevantAttrs
   624→	return destroyPlan, evalScope, diags
   625→}

   628→	explicitMoveStmts := refactoring.FindMoveStatements(config)

The fix is on line 105: `s2.RootOutputValues[k]` → `s.RootOutputValues[k]`.

The bug was that `RootOutputValuesEqual` was comparing each value in `s2` against itself (always true), instead of comparing against the corresponding value in `s`. This caused `terraform plan -refresh-only` to always consider output values unchanged, since the applyability check at `context_plan.go` relies on `!plan.PriorState.RootOutputValuesEqual(plan.PrevRunState)` returning `false` (meaning they differ) to mark the plan as applyable.
